_source: HVAC Excellence standards, ASHRAE Handbook, EPA 608 certification materials, Refrigeration troubleshooting guides
_note: Long-tail HVAC/refrigeration knowledge. Refrigeration cycle, diagnostics, EPA regulations, psychrometrics, load calculations.

basic_principles.four_main_components: Compressor, condenser, metering device (TXV or piston), evaporator
basic_principles.phase_changes: Liquid refrigerant evaporates (absorbs heat) in evaporator; vapor condenses (rejects heat) in condenser
basic_principles.pressure_temperature_relationship: Higher pressure = higher saturation temperature; AC works by manipulating pressure to control phase change temperatures
basic_principles.superheat_subcool: Superheat = vapor temp above saturation temp; Subcool = liquid temp below saturation temp; critical for charge verification

compressor.function: Pumps refrigerant; creates low pressure (suction) and high pressure (discharge); heart of system

types.reciprocating: Piston-driven; residential AC, small commercial; 1-10 ton; noisy but reliable
types.scroll: Two spiral scrolls; quieter; residential/light commercial; 1.5-25 ton; fewer parts, higher efficiency
types.rotary: Rotating vane or screw; commercial chillers; 20-500+ ton; continuous compression
types.centrifugal: High-speed impeller; large buildings; 100-10,000 ton; most efficient for large loads

compressor.suction_pressure: Low side; 60-80 PSIG typical for R-410A AC (varies with ambient); feeds compressor
compressor.discharge_pressure: High side; 200-350 PSIG typical for R-410A AC (varies with ambient); exits compressor to condenser

condenser.function: Rejects heat to outdoors; high-pressure vapor enters, liquid exits; heat removed = heat absorbed at evaporator + compressor work

types.air_cooled: Fan blows air over coil; residential, small commercial; ambient dependent; cheaper, easier maintenance
types.water_cooled: Water flows through tubes; large commercial; more efficient; requires cooling tower or water source
types.evaporative: Water spray on coil; combines air and evaporation cooling; mid-size commercial; water usage concern

condenser.subcooling: 10-20°F typical; liquid line temp vs. saturation temp at condensing pressure; verifies full condensation and charge adequacy
condenser.fouling_impact: Dirty condenser coil = high head pressure = reduced capacity, efficiency; wash coils annually

metering_device.function: Reduces refrigerant pressure; controls refrigerant flow into evaporator; creates pressure differential

txv_thermostatic_expansion_valve.operation: Modulates based on superheat; sensing bulb on suction line; maintains constant superheat (8-12°F typical)
txv_thermostatic_expansion_valve.advantages: Efficient at varying loads; maintains performance; prevents flooding or starving evaporator
txv_thermostatic_expansion_valve.components: Sensing bulb, diaphragm, needle and seat, adjustment screw
txv_thermostatic_expansion_valve.problems: Bulb loses charge (starves evaporator), stuck closed (high superheat), stuck open (low superheat, flooding)

piston_fixed_orifice.operation: Fixed hole size; refrigerant flow varies with pressure difference; simple, cheap
piston_fixed_orifice.advantages: No moving parts; reliable; low cost
piston_fixed_orifice.disadvantages: Less efficient at part load; charge-critical; not ideal for heat pumps (need bi-flow piston)
piston_fixed_orifice.sizing: Matched to system tonnage; wrong size = poor performance; color-coded

eev_electronic_expansion_valve.operation: Stepper motor controls opening; microprocessor-controlled; can optimize for varying conditions
eev_electronic_expansion_valve.advantages: Most efficient; precise control; diagnostic feedback

evaporator.function: Absorbs heat indoors; low-pressure liquid enters, vapor exits; provides cooling effect

types.a_coil: Upright A-shape; fits above furnace; residential; compact
types.horizontal: Flat in attic or crawlspace; space constraints
types.cased_uncased: Cased has cabinet/drain pan built-in; uncased for custom installations

evaporator.superheat: 8-12°F typical for TXV; 10-20°F for piston; suction line temp vs. saturation temp at suction pressure; verifies proper evaporation and no flooding
evaporator.airflow: 400 CFM per ton standard; 350 for humid, 450 for dry climates; low airflow = low suction pressure, icing; high airflow = high superheat, reduced dehumidification
evaporator.icing_causes: Low airflow (dirty filter, closed registers, bad blower), low charge, dirty evaporator coil, low outdoor temp (<55°F), refrigerant restriction

common_types.r_22: HCFC; phased out 2020; legacy systems only; no new production; reclaimed stock available
common_types.r_410a: HFC; residential AC/heat pumps; higher pressure than R-22 (350 vs 250 PSIG); incompatible with R-22 systems; global warming potential concerns
common_types.r_32: HFC; lower GWP than R-410A; emerging residential; fewer countries adopted; higher flammability (A2L classification)
common_types.r_134a: HFC; automotive AC, chillers; lower pressure; GWP concerns; being replaced by R-1234yf
common_types.r_404a_r_507: HFC; commercial refrigeration; medium/low temp; high GWP; being phased down
common_types.r_744_co2: Natural refrigerant; low GWP; transcritical systems; high pressure (1400+ PSIG); emerging commercial refrigeration

epa_608_certification.type_i: Small appliances (<5 lbs charge); window AC, dehumidifiers, small refrigerators
epa_608_certification.type_ii: High-pressure systems; AC, heat pumps, R-410A, R-134a systems
epa_608_certification.type_iii: Low-pressure systems; chillers using R-123, centrifugal compressors
epa_608_certification.universal: All of the above; most HVAC techs get universal
epa_608_certification.requirements: Illegal to vent refrigerants; must recover to EPA levels; Type I 90%, Type II/III 0 PSIG or 10" Hg vacuum
epa_608_certification.leak_repair: Commercial systems >50 lbs: if annual leak rate >30% (comfort cooling) or >20% (commercial refrigeration), must repair within 30 days

pressure_temperature_charts.usage: Convert gauge pressure to saturation temperature; essential for superheat/subcool calculations
pressure_temperature_charts.example_r410a: 118 PSIG suction = 40°F saturation; if suction line is 50°F, superheat = 10°F; 298 PSIG liquid line = 110°F saturation; if liquid line is 95°F, subcool = 15°F
pressure_temperature_charts.rule: Each refrigerant has unique PT curve; always use correct chart; mobile apps available

handling_safety.never_mix: Different refrigerants contaminate each other; ruins recovery cylinders; separate tanks for each type
handling_safety.ventilation: Refrigerants heavier than air; displace oxygen in confined spaces; ensure ventilation
handling_safety.open_flame: Refrigerants decompose into phosgene gas (toxic) near flame; use electronic leak detectors, not torch
handling_safety.liquid_contact: Frostbite risk; wear gloves and safety glasses when handling; evaporates quickly removing heat from skin
handling_safety.recovery_cylinders: DOT approved; color-coded; never fill above 80%; check certification dates

superheat_method.when_to_use: Fixed orifice (piston) metering devices; critical charge systems
procedure: 1. Measure suction line temperature at compressor (insulated section)
2. Measure suction pressure; convert to saturation temp using PT chart
3. Superheat = Suction line temp - Saturation temp
4. Compare to target superheat (10-20°F typical, varies by outdoor temp and indoor humidity)
superheat_method.target_superheat_calculation: Use manufacturer chart or formula: Target SH = (outdoor temp - indoor wet bulb) / 2.5; typical range 10-18°F
superheat_method.adjustments: Low superheat = overcharged, remove refrigerant; High superheat = undercharged, add refrigerant

subcool_method.when_to_use: TXV metering devices; systems with receiver; critical for charge verification
procedure: 1. Measure liquid line temperature (before metering device, after condenser)
2. Measure high-side (liquid line) pressure; convert to saturation temp using PT chart
3. Subcool = Saturation temp - Liquid line temp
4. Compare to target subcool (10-20°F typical, manufacturer specifies)
subcool_method.target: Usually 10-15°F for R-410A; check nameplate or installation manual
subcool_method.adjustments: Low subcool = undercharged; High subcool = overcharged

weigh_in_method.when_to_use: Exact charge known from nameplate; complete evacuation done; factory charge (ductless mini-splits)
weigh_in_method.procedure: Evacuate system completely; weigh in exact charge using refrigerant scale; most accurate method
weigh_in_method.advantages: Eliminates guesswork; works even if superheat/subcool can't be measured
weigh_in_method.limitations: Requires knowing exact charge; doesn't account for line set length variations unless specified

common_mistakes.charging_liquid_into_compressor: Causes slugging, damages compressor; always charge liquid into high side (when off) or vapor into low side (when running)
common_mistakes.not_accounting_for_conditions: Superheat/subcool targets change with outdoor temp; 95°F day vs 75°F day = different readings even with correct charge
common_mistakes.charging_into_iced_coil: Defrost first; ice blocks airflow giving false low pressure readings
common_mistakes.ignoring_airflow: Fix airflow issues first before adjusting charge; dirty filter or low CFM mimics low charge

causes: Low charge (most common); verify with superheat/subcool
Restriction (filter drier, metering device, kinked line); subcool may be high, frost before restriction
Low airflow over evaporator (dirty filter, iced coil, bad blower); high superheat, iced coil
Low outdoor temperature (<55°F); not a fault, system not designed for low ambient
Compressor weak/worn (low pumping capacity); motor amps may be low
low_suction_pressure.symptoms: Reduced cooling, longer run times, possible evaporator icing, high superheat (if low charge/restriction)

causes: Overcharge; low superheat, high subcool
Metering device stuck open or oversized; flooding evaporator, low superheat
High load on evaporator (outdoor air entering, undersized unit); normal response
Compressor valves leaking (inefficient compression); low discharge pressure also
high_suction_pressure.symptoms: Insufficient cooling, compressor runs hot, possible liquid slugging if severe

causes: Low charge; low suction pressure also, high superheat
Compressor valves worn/leaking; low compression efficiency, low suction pressure
Metering device stuck open; flooding evaporator
low_discharge_pressure.symptoms: Poor cooling, compressor may run cooler than normal, reduced capacity

causes: Dirty condenser coil (most common); clean coil, check fan operation
Condenser fan not running; check capacitor, motor, wiring, control board
Overcharge; high subcool (>20°F), liquid backing into condenser
Non-condensables in system (air); purge from high side, pull vacuum if significant
Restricted airflow (condenser blocked, recirculation)
high_discharge_pressure.symptoms: High amp draw, compressor overheating, safety cutout tripping, reduced capacity and efficiency

causes: Low charge; pressure cutout or overload trips, cools, resets, repeats
Dirty filters/coils; overheating, thermal overload trips
Oversized unit; satisfies thermostat too quickly, inefficient, poor dehumidification
Thermostat location or differential issue; in direct sun, near supply register
Failing capacitor; hard start, high amps, thermal overload trips
Low voltage; check supply voltage under load, should be within 10% of nameplate
compressor_short_cycling.symptoms: Frequent on-off, never runs long cycles, high electric bills, poor comfort

causes: Bad capacitor (most common); bulging, leaking oil, test with multimeter (should be within 6% of rating)
Seized compressor; ohm terminals (should show resistance, not open/short), try hard start kit
Contactor not pulling in; 24V control voltage present?, coil energized?, contacts pitted/worn?
Thermal overload open; compressor too hot, wait 1-2 hours to reset; if repeats, find cause (low airflow, low charge, bad capacitor)
Low voltage; check supply at disconnect; brownout conditions; undersized wire; loose connections
Control board not sending call; check thermostat, wiring, transformer, board
compressor_won_t_start.troubleshooting: Ohm compressor terminals: C-R, C-S, R-S should all show resistance (usually 1-20 ohms); C-R + C-S should equal R-S; any short to ground = bad compressor

refrigerant_restrictions.symptoms: Frost/ice before restriction; pressure drop across restriction; subcool may be high; capacity reduced
refrigerant_restrictions.locations: Filter drier (most common if recently replaced or system contaminated), liquid line, TXV screen, cap tube
refrigerant_restrictions.diagnosis: Temperature difference across component >10°F = restriction; feel line for sudden temp drop
refrigerant_restrictions.fix: Replace filter drier; if TXV screen, clean or replace valve; if cap tube, may need to replace metering device

properties_of_air.dry_bulb_temperature: Actual air temperature; what thermometer reads; sensible heat
properties_of_air.wet_bulb_temperature: Temperature with evaporative cooling; wet wick on thermometer; approaches dry bulb as humidity rises
properties_of_air.dew_point: Temperature at which moisture condenses; 100% RH at dew point; can't cool below without dehumidifying
properties_of_air.relative_humidity: % of moisture in air vs. saturation at that temperature; 50% RH comfortable; >60% feels muggy
properties_of_air.humidity_ratio: Grains of moisture per pound of dry air; 7000 grains = 1 lb water; absolute moisture content

psychrometric_chart.usage: Plot dry bulb and wet bulb to find all other properties; essential for load calculations and system design
psychrometric_chart.total_cooling: Sensible (temp reduction) + Latent (moisture removal); both shown on chart
psychrometric_chart.process_lines: Cooling and dehumidifying = down and left on chart; heating = horizontal right; humidifying = vertical up

sensible_heat_ratio.definition: SHR = Sensible cooling / Total cooling; 1.0 = all sensible (dry heat), 0.6 = 60% sensible 40% latent (moisture)
sensible_heat_ratio.typical_values: 0.7-0.8 for comfort cooling; 0.95+ for computer room AC (little moisture load)
sensible_heat_ratio.equipment_selection: Match equipment SHR to building SHR; oversized unit = poor dehumidification (short cycles before removing moisture)

manual_j.purpose: ACCA standard for residential load calculation; determines proper equipment size
manual_j.inputs: Building dimensions, window area and orientation, insulation R-values, infiltration, occupancy, internal gains (lights, appliances), climate data
manual_j.outputs: Heating and cooling loads in BTU/hr; room-by-room breakdown; equipment sizing
manual_j.importance: Prevents oversizing (short cycling, poor dehumidification, higher cost) and undersizing (inadequate comfort)
manual_j.software: Wrightsoft, Elite Software, Manual J calculators; hand calc possible but tedious

rules_of_thumb.caution: Rules of thumb are rough estimates only; always use Manual J for actual installations
rules_of_thumb.residential_cooling: 400-600 sq ft per ton; 400 in hot/humid, 600 in moderate climates; assumes 8 ft ceilings, moderate insulation
rules_of_thumb.residential_heating: 25-40 BTU/hr per sq ft; 40 in cold climates, 25 in mild; fuel type and efficiency matter
rules_of_thumb.oversizing_problems: Short cycling, temperature swings, poor humidity control, higher equipment cost, reduced efficiency, shorter equipment life

heat_gain_sources.solar: Windows, especially west-facing; biggest afternoon load; shading and low-E glass reduce
heat_gain_sources.conduction: Through walls, roof, floor; insulation R-value determines; roof is biggest in summer
heat_gain_sources.infiltration: Air leaks; cracks, gaps, poor sealing; ducts in attic/crawlspace lose 20-30% if unsealed
heat_gain_sources.internal_gains: People (~400 BTU/hr each), lights (3.4 BTU/hr per watt), appliances (kitchen major source)
heat_gain_sources.ventilation: Outdoor air for IAQ; load on system; ERV/HRV reduce load

single_phase_residential.voltage: 120V (lights, receptacles, blower), 240V (compressor, heat strips); split-phase from transformer
single_phase_residential.circuit_requirements: Dedicated circuit for AC; 15-60A typical (depends on tonnage); disconnect required within sight
single_phase_residential.wire_sizing: Based on amp draw; 14 AWG for 15A, 12 AWG for 20A, 10 AWG for 30A; follow NEC Table 310.16
single_phase_residential.overcurrent_protection: Breaker sized per manufacturer (usually max overcurrent protection on nameplate); must not exceed

compressor_circuits.contactor: 24V coil energizes, closes contacts, sends 240V to compressor; contacts rated 20-40A typical

capacitors.run_capacitor: Stays in circuit while running; improves efficiency and torque; measured in µF (microfarads); should be within 6% of rating
capacitors.start_capacitor: High µF (100-400); only during startup; potential relay or solid-state relay kicks out after start; hard start kit
capacitors.dual_capacitor: Combined run cap for compressor and fan in one housing; common terminal (C), compressor (HERM), fan

compressor_circuits.overload_protector: Thermal or current-sensing; mounted on compressor; opens if overheating or high amps; auto-reset when cools

control_voltage.24vac: Standard HVAC control voltage; transformer steps down 120V to 24V; powers thermostat, contactors, relays, gas valve
control_voltage.r_wire: Red; 24V hot from transformer; powers thermostat
control_voltage.c_wire: Common; return path to transformer; required for smart thermostats (most older stats used battery backup, didn't need C)
control_voltage.y_wire: Yellow; cooling call; energizes compressor contactor
control_voltage.g_wire: Green; fan call; energizes blower relay
control_voltage.w_wire: White; heating call; energizes heat source (furnace, heat strips, heat pump reversing valve)

motor_troubleshooting.blower_motor_amps: Check nameplate FLA (full load amps); should run at or below; if high, check capacitor, bearing, duct restriction
motor_troubleshooting.fan_motor_amps: Condenser fan; if high, check capacitor, blade damage, bearing; if low, check voltage, winding resistance
motor_troubleshooting.voltage_checks: Measure at contactor under load; should be within 10% of nameplate; low voltage = high amps, overheating
motor_troubleshooting.capacitor_testing: Discharge first (short across terminals with insulated screwdriver); test with multimeter capacitance setting; ±6% acceptable

evacuation.purpose: Remove air and moisture from system; air causes high head pressure, moisture causes acid and ice
procedure: 1. Connect vacuum pump to both service ports
2. Open service valves, start pump, pull to 500 microns or below
3. Close valves, observe vacuum hold (should not rise >1000 microns in 10 min)
4. If doesn't hold, leak present; find and repair before charging
evacuation.micron_gauge: Measures deep vacuum; 500 microns = good evacuation; 50,000 microns = atmospheric pressure
evacuation.time_required: Minimum 30-45 min for residential; longer for larger systems or if moisture present; use large hoses (3/8" or 1/2")

methods.electronic_leak_detector: Most sensitive; heated diode or infrared sensor; can detect 0.1 oz/year; check all joints, valves, service ports
methods.soap_bubbles: Cheap, visual; works for large leaks; spray fittings, look for bubbles; not sensitive enough for small leaks
methods.nitrogen_pressure_test: Pressurize to 150-300 PSI with dry nitrogen; listen for hiss; soap test; hold pressure 30+ min
methods.uv_dye: Add to system, run, inspect with UV light; finds leaks over time; good for elusive leaks; must recover refrigerant to repair

leak_detection.common_leak_locations: Schrader valve cores (tighten or replace), flare fittings (loosen, re-flare, re-tighten), brazed joints (pinhole from improper nitrogen flow), vibration points (liquid line at condenser)

brazing.vs_soldering: Brazing uses filler metal >840°F (silver-phos alloy); stronger joint; required for refrigerant lines; soldering <840°F, not strong enough
procedure: 1. Clean tube with emery cloth; remove oxidation
2. Apply flux if using brass/bronze filler (silver-phos is self-fluxing on copper)
3. Flow nitrogen through line at 1-2 PSI while brazing (prevents oxidation inside tube)
4. Heat joint evenly with torch; when hot enough, filler will flow into joint by capillary action
5. Let cool naturally; do not water-quench (weakens joint)
brazing.nitrogen_flow: Critical; prevents black scale (copper oxide) inside tubing which can clog metering device and contaminate oil
brazing.filler_metals: SilFos 15% (copper-to-copper, no flux needed), SilFos 5% (copper-to-brass), 45% silver for dissimilar metals

oil_management.oil_types: Mineral oil (R-22), POE polyolester (R-410A), PVE (R-32); never mix; wrong oil = compressor failure
oil_management.oil_return: Oil circulates with refrigerant; must return to compressor; ensure line velocities adequate; trap oil in suction risers
oil_management.acid_test: If compressor burnout, oil becomes acidic; acid test kit; must replace oil and filter drier; flush if severe
oil_management.oil_level_check: Sight glass on compressor (if equipped); 1/4 to 3/4 full typical; too high = slugging, too low = lack of lubrication
